groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Project, audience, funding, rewards, creator context, output language, and must-follow requirements needed for a useful first campaign draft.

groups.title: Story & Positioning
groups.description: Origin, problem, solution, differentiation, current progress, credibility, audience motivation, and approved evidence.

groups.title: Rewards & Funding
groups.description: Reward format, limited rewards, shipping, budget, stretch goals, campaign duration, and launch timing.

groups.title: Delivery & Risk
groups.description: Production, milestones, fulfillment, suppliers, risks, mitigation, and prototype or manufacturing evidence.

groups.title: Voice, Media & Compliance
groups.description: Campaign tone, narrative voice, media assets, video output, sources, sensitive claims, evidence support, and restrictions.

variables.label: Project name
variables.placeholder: Example: Foldlight Modular Desk Lamp
variables.hint: Enter the public name of the project or product.

help.what: Provide the project name before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: The project name anchors campaign titles, story copy, rewards, and calls to action.
help.example: Good example: Foldlight Modular Desk Lamp
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported promises, fake urgency, fake scarcity, or changing the campaign into a different project.

variables.label: Project category
variables.placeholder: Choose the closest category
variables.hint: Select the category that best matches what you are creating.

help.what: Provide the project category before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: Category affects campaign structure, proof expectations, risks, terminology, and backer questions.
help.example: Good example: Design & Technology
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported promises, fake urgency, fake scarcity, or changing the campaign into a different project.

variables.label: What are you creating?
variables.placeholder: Describe the project, what it does or contains, and what backers will help bring to life.
variables.hint: Focus on the concrete project rather than general company promotion.

help.what: Provide the what are you creating? before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: The model cannot safely infer the project’s function, scope, format, or deliverable.
help.example: Good example: A modular desk lamp with adjustable light panels for small creative workspaces.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported promises, fake urgency, fake scarcity, or changing the campaign into a different project.

variables.label: Core project promise
variables.placeholder: What should a backer understand or feel after seeing the campaign?
variables.hint: Use one clear benefit, creative promise, or main takeaway.

help.what: Optional context for the core project promise field.
help.why: It helps unify the campaign around one memorable backer-facing message.
help.example: Good example: Help backers build a calmer, better-lit workspace without replacing their whole desk setup.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Primary backers
variables.placeholder: Describe the people most likely to care about and support this project.
variables.hint: Include relevant interests, needs, experience level, or community context.

help.what: Provide the primary backers before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: Audience knowledge materially changes positioning, language, proof, rewards, and FAQ topics.
help.example: Good example: Design-conscious remote workers, students, and creators who need flexible lighting in small rooms.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported promises, fake urgency, fake scarcity, or changing the campaign into a different project.

variables.label: Funding goal
variables.placeholder: Example: 25000
variables.hint: Enter the intended Kickstarter funding goal without currency symbols.

help.what: Provide the funding goal before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: The campaign must explain what the requested amount makes possible without inventing a target.
help.example: Good example: 25000
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: Funding currency
variables.placeholder: Example: USD, EUR, GBP or CAD
variables.hint: Use the currency selected in the Kickstarter project editor.

help.what: Provide the funding currency before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: Funding and reward amounts must use a consistent, user-confirmed currency.
help.example: Good example: USD
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: What will funding pay for?
variables.placeholder: Explain the main production, development, printing, manufacturing, or fulfillment needs.
variables.hint: Include only real planned uses of funds.

help.what: Provide the what will funding pay for? before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: Backers need a credible explanation of why funding is necessary and how it supports delivery.
help.example: Good example: Tooling, first production run, quality checks, packaging, and fulfillment preparation.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported promises, fake urgency, fake scarcity, or changing the campaign into a different project.

variables.label: Reward tiers
variables.placeholder: List each reward with its pledge amount, contents, limits, and delivery estimate when known.
variables.hint: One reward per line is sufficient. Do not include unconfirmed prices or dates.

help.what: Provide the reward tiers before generating the Kickstarter campaign.
help.why: Rewards determine campaign value, funding logic, fulfillment expectations, and FAQ content.
help.example: Good example: $10 Supporter update pack; $49 Early lamp kit; $79 Standard lamp kit; estimated delivery Q2 2027.
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: Creator or team
variables.placeholder: Describe the creator, team, studio, or organization behind the project.
variables.hint: Use only public, approved identity and experience details.

help.what: Optional context for the creator or team field.
help.why: Kickstarter backers need to understand who is responsible for the project.
help.example: Good example: A small industrial-design team with experience prototyping home-office accessories.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Current project stage
variables.placeholder: Choose the current stage
variables.hint: Be honest about whether this is a concept, prototype, production-ready project, or new run.

help.what: Optional context for the current project stage field.
help.why: Stage controls proof, risk wording, timeline confidence, and Design & Technology review concerns.
help.example: Good example: Prototype or early version
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

options: Funding a new edition or run

variables.label: Output language
variables.placeholder: Example: English, Ukrainian, German, or Spanish
variables.hint: Enter the language for the generated campaign package.

help.what: Optional context for the output language field.
help.why: The campaign copy should be generated in the audience-facing language.
help.example: Good example: English
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Additional requirements
variables.placeholder: Add remaining instructions, must-use wording, exclusions, or launch constraints.
variables.hint: Use only for requirements not already captured in another field.

help.what: Optional context for the additional requirements field.
help.why: This keeps edge cases controlled without adding unnecessary form fields.
help.example: Good example: No fake urgency, no invented reviews, and no delivery dates beyond supplied estimates.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: How the project began
variables.placeholder: Explain the real moment, need, observation, or creative spark behind the project.
variables.hint: Leave blank if the origin story is not public or not relevant.

help.what: Optional context for the how the project began field.
help.why: A truthful origin story can make the campaign feel creator-led and specific.
help.example: Good example: The project started after the team struggled to keep a shared apartment workspace comfortable after dark.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Problem or opportunity
variables.placeholder: Describe the real need, frustration, creative opportunity, or gap the project addresses.
variables.hint: Avoid exaggerating the problem or turning it into fear-based copy.

help.what: Optional context for the problem or opportunity field.
help.why: The story needs a credible reason for backers to care.
help.example: Good example: Small desks often need focused light without bulky lamps, exposed cables, or harsh glare.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: How the project answers it
variables.placeholder: Describe how the project works, what it contains, or how it responds to the need.
variables.hint: Use factual, project-specific details rather than generic benefits.

help.what: Optional context for the how the project answers it field.
help.why: This turns the summary into a clear campaign narrative.
help.example: Good example: Interchangeable panels let backers direct light for reading, sketching, video calls, or ambient work.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: What makes it distinctive?
variables.placeholder: List only confirmed differences, design choices, creative angle, or production approach.
variables.hint: Do not invent superiority, awards, or unsupported comparisons.

help.what: Optional context for the what makes it distinctive? field.
help.why: Differentiation helps positioning without relying on hype.
help.example: Good example: Flat-pack design, replaceable panels, repairable hinges, and warm/cool light presets.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Progress so far
variables.placeholder: List prototypes, drafts, samples, testing, partnerships, production steps, or completed work.
variables.hint: Separate confirmed progress from future plans.

help.what: Optional context for the progress so far field.
help.why: Current progress determines credibility and risk framing.
help.example: Good example: Two working prototypes have been assembled and tested for brightness, hinge stability, and desk fit.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Relevant experience
variables.placeholder: Add approved skills, past work, shipped projects, production experience, or collaborators.
variables.hint: Do not add credentials or achievements that cannot be published.

help.what: Optional context for the relevant experience field.
help.why: Backers need evidence that the creator can reasonably attempt the project.
help.example: Good example: The team has previously shipped small-batch desk accessories through a local maker studio.
help.avoid: Avoid invented evidence, credentials, media, supplier relationships, approvals, testimonials, test results, links, or sources.

variables.label: Why will people back it?
variables.placeholder: Explain why the audience may want to support this project now.
variables.hint: Use practical, creative, emotional, community, or collector motivations that are actually supported.

help.what: Optional context for the why will people back it? field.
help.why: Backer motivation shapes the campaign opening, reward positioning, and CTA.
help.example: Good example: Backers want a practical tool that also feels intentional and good-looking in a visible workspace.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Evidence and validation
variables.placeholder: Add approved prototype evidence, test notes, community feedback, press, samples, or data.
variables.hint: Only include proof that really exists and may be used publicly.

help.what: Optional context for the evidence and validation field.
help.why: Evidence strengthens credibility while preventing invented social proof.
help.example: Good example: Prototype photos, brightness tests, and notes from five informal workspace trials are available.
help.avoid: Avoid invented evidence, credentials, media, supplier relationships, approvals, testimonials, test results, links, or sources.

variables.label: Main reward format
variables.placeholder: Choose the closest reward format
variables.hint: Select the dominant reward type or let the model infer it from the supplied tiers.

help.what: Optional context for the main reward format field.
help.why: Reward format controls shipping, fulfillment, access, and risk language.
help.example: Good example: Physical
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Limited or early rewards
variables.placeholder: List confirmed limited quantities, early-bird tiers, or availability rules.
variables.hint: Leave blank if limits are not configured and verified.

help.what: Optional context for the limited or early rewards field.
help.why: Limited rewards can be mentioned only when the user supplies real limits.
help.example: Good example: Early lamp kit is limited to 200 units only if that quantity is confirmed in the reward setup.
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: Shipping plan
variables.placeholder: List shipping regions, costs, timing assumptions, taxes, customs, VAT, or local pickup notes.
variables.hint: Shown only for physical or mixed rewards.

help.what: Optional context for the shipping plan field.
help.why: Shipping details materially affect reward clarity and fulfillment risk.
help.example: Good example: Ships to the United States, Canada, EU, and United Kingdom; customs and VAT collected where applicable if confirmed.
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: Budget breakdown
variables.placeholder: Explain planned use of funds by category when known.
variables.hint: Use exact categories only when they are real; otherwise provide narrative context.

help.what: Optional context for the budget breakdown field.
help.why: Backers often need a transparent explanation of how funds support delivery.
help.example: Good example: Tooling, materials, assembly, testing, packaging, freight, fulfillment, and contingency.
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: Stretch goals
variables.placeholder: List only confirmed stretch goals and what they unlock after the base project is viable.
variables.hint: Leave blank if stretch goals are not defined.

help.what: Optional context for the stretch goals field.
help.why: Stretch goals should not distract from or endanger the base project.
help.example: Good example: Additional color panels only after the base lamp is funded and production remains feasible.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Campaign duration
variables.placeholder: Example: 30
variables.hint: Enter a planned duration from 1 to 60 days.

help.what: Optional context for the campaign duration field.
help.why: Kickstarter campaign timing affects deadline wording and planning notes.
help.example: Good example: 30
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Planned launch date
variables.hint: Enter an ISO-style date when the launch date is known. Use YYYY-MM-DD when possible.

help.what: Optional context for the planned launch date field.
help.why: A launch date plus duration can support a tentative end date without inventing timing.
help.example: Good example: 2026-09-15
help.avoid: Avoid unconfirmed prices, quantities, delivery dates, funding details, taxes, customs terms, limits, or scarcity claims.

variables.label: Production plan
variables.placeholder: Describe how the project will be made after the campaign closes.
variables.hint: Include production, development, printing, manufacturing, assembly, QA, or creative work.

help.what: Optional context for the production plan field.
help.why: Production detail helps make the campaign credible and backer-safe.
help.example: Good example: Finalize tooling, order components, assemble pilot run, complete quality checks, package units, and begin fulfillment.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Timeline and milestones
variables.placeholder: List the major steps from campaign close through production and delivery.
variables.hint: Use approximate milestones when dates are not confirmed.

help.what: Optional context for the timeline and milestones field.
help.why: Milestones let the output explain progress without inventing exact dates.
help.example: Good example: Tooling after campaign close, pilot run, production batch, QC, packaging, regional fulfillment.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Fulfillment plan
variables.placeholder: Explain delivery method, access process, fulfillment partners, pickup, or digital delivery.
variables.hint: Include only confirmed delivery arrangements.

help.what: Optional context for the fulfillment plan field.
help.why: Fulfillment details shape rewards, FAQ, shipping notes, and risks.
help.example: Good example: Use a regional fulfillment partner for packed lamp kits and send tracking when available.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Partners and suppliers
variables.placeholder: List confirmed suppliers, manufacturers, collaborators, printers, studios, or fulfillment partners.
variables.hint: Do not imply contracts, endorsements, certifications, or capacity not supplied.

help.what: Optional context for the partners and suppliers field.
help.why: Partner and supplier context affects production credibility and risk.
help.example: Good example: Confirmed hinge supplier and packaging vendor; final assembly partner under review.
help.avoid: Avoid invented evidence, credentials, media, supplier relationships, approvals, testimonials, test results, links, or sources.

variables.label: Known risks
variables.placeholder: List realistic project, production, supplier, software, licensing, customs, or scheduling risks.
variables.hint: Be candid; do not promise that uncertainty is eliminated.

help.what: Optional context for the known risks field.
help.why: Kickstarter requires honest risk communication rather than guaranteed delivery.
help.example: Good example: Tooling delays, component lead times, color consistency, freight changes, and quality-control rework.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Risk mitigation
variables.placeholder: Explain how the creator will reduce, monitor, communicate, or respond to known risks.
variables.hint: Use concrete plans rather than generic reassurance.

help.what: Optional context for the risk mitigation field.
help.why: Risk sections are stronger when each meaningful risk has an actual response plan.
help.example: Good example: Buffer production time, order sample parts before full production, communicate delays monthly, and hold a small contingency reserve.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Prototype and production proof
variables.placeholder: Add working prototype evidence, manufacturing readiness, samples, tests, or supplier proof.
variables.hint: Shown for Design & Technology projects.

help.what: Optional context for the prototype and production proof field.
help.why: Design and technology campaigns need clear evidence of real project progress.
help.example: Good example: Working prototype photos, hinge stress notes, brightness test chart, and supplier sample invoices.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Campaign tone
variables.placeholder: Select a tone
variables.hint: Choose the tone the public campaign should use.

help.what: Optional context for the campaign tone field.
help.why: Tone controls how the story, risks, CTA, and creator voice feel to backers.
help.example: Good example: Authentic and confident
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Narrative voice
variables.placeholder: Select the narrative voice
variables.hint: Choose whose voice the campaign should feel like.

help.what: Optional context for the narrative voice field.
help.why: Kickstarter campaigns need a credible public voice rather than generic marketing copy.
help.example: Good example: Creator-led and conversational
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Available media
variables.placeholder: List available photos, prototypes, diagrams, samples, demos, charts, videos, or team images.
variables.hint: Do not imply media exists unless it is supplied.

help.what: Optional context for the available media field.
help.why: Media notes help place existing assets without inventing footage or proof.
help.example: Good example: Prototype photos, exploded-view diagram, desk setup shots, short assembly clip, and team photo.
help.avoid: Avoid invented evidence, credentials, media, supplier relationships, approvals, testimonials, test results, links, or sources.

variables.label: Campaign video output
variables.placeholder: Select video output
variables.hint: Choose whether to generate a full script, short script, outline, or omit the video section.

help.what: Optional context for the campaign video output field.
help.why: Video output should match the user’s actual campaign needs and available media.
help.example: Good example: Full 2–3 minute script
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake social proof, fake urgency, pressure, hidden assumptions, or unsupported Kickstarter claims.

variables.label: Sources and reference content
variables.placeholder: Paste approved source notes, links, specs, drafts, references, or research.
variables.hint: Use source materials as factual reference; do not copy long copyrighted passages.

help.what: Optional context for the sources and reference content field.
help.why: Source material improves accuracy and helps avoid unsupported claims.
help.example: Good example: Paste approved product notes, prototype data, reward details, budget notes, and source links.
help.avoid: Avoid invented evidence, credentials, media, supplier relationships, approvals, testimonials, test results, links, or sources.

variables.label: Sensitive claim area
variables.placeholder: Select if the project touches a sensitive area
variables.hint: Use when claims involve health, finance, legal, safety, environmental, political, child-related, or regulated topics.

help.what: Optional context for the sensitive claim area field.
help.why: Sensitive areas require stricter claim handling and review notes.
help.example: Good example: None
help.avoid: Avoid medical, legal, financial, safety, environmental, political, or child-related claims without supplied support and review.

variables.label: Evidence for sensitive claims
variables.placeholder: Provide approved evidence, review notes, disclaimers, or limits for sensitive claims.
variables.hint: Shown only when a sensitive claim area is selected.

help.what: Optional context for the evidence for sensitive claims field.
help.why: High-stakes claims need explicit support or must be flagged for review.
help.example: Good example: Approved testing notes, certification status, legal review notes, or claims to avoid.
help.avoid: Avoid invented evidence, credentials, media, supplier relationships, approvals, testimonials, test results, links, or sources.

variables.label: Claims and content to avoid
variables.placeholder: List words, claims, promises, topics, proof points, or positioning to avoid.
variables.hint: Use this for legal, brand, platform, rights, privacy, or factual limits.

help.what: Optional context for the claims and content to avoid field.
help.why: Restrictions prevent unsupported claims, rights issues, and misleading campaign copy.
help.example: Good example: Do not claim waterproofing, medical benefits, certified safety approval, existing backers, or guaranteed delivery.
help.avoid: Avoid medical, legal, financial, safety, environmental, political, or child-related claims without supplied support and review.
